executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment totaled 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing year-over-year enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. California leads with 12.6 million enrollees despite a significant 6.2% decline, while other large states showed more moderate decreases: Texas declined 3.1% to 4.0 million enrollees, New York decreased 1.8% to 6.5 million, and Florida dropped 4.0% to 3.6 million. Notably, North Carolina bucked the national trend with a 0.5% enrollment increase to 2.9 million beneficiaries. National program spending reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a federal share of $142.0 billion and an average per-enrollee cost of $2,869. Per-enrollee spending varies dramatically across states, ranging from Florida's efficient $2,031 per beneficiary to Pennsylvania's high-cost $4,377 per enrollee, with most large states clustering between $2,600-$3,500.

Texas demonstrates mixed performance compared to peer states, maintaining the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with relatively moderate per-enrollee costs of $2,988—higher than California ($2,280) and North Carolina ($2,616) but lower than New York ($3,431) and Ohio ($3,561). However, Texas ranks 30th nationally in quality measures with a score of 62 out of100, placing it in the "moderate" performance tier but trailing several comparable states. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, covering 3.9 million of its 4.0 million enrollees. Current regulatory developments include new federal enrollment moratoria and upcoming 2028 Home and Community-Based Services quality standards, which may impact state program operations and require policy adjustments to maintain compliance while managing costs effectively.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the third-largest Medicaid program nationally with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a substantial 5.4% of total national enrollment. The state's per-enrollee spending of $2,988 sits just above the national average of $2,869, demonstrating relatively efficient cost management compared to peer large states like New York ($3,431/enrollee) and Pennsylvania ($4,377/enrollee). Among non-expansion southern states, Texas outperforms Florida's remarkably low $2,031 per-enrollee spending while maintaining similar enrollment trends, with both states experiencing moderate year-over-year enrollment declines (-3.1% for Texas vs. -4.0% for Florida) as pandemic-era continuous enrollment policies wind down. Texas's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong infrastructure for coordinated care delivery that covers nearly all beneficiaries.

However, Texas faces notable quality challenges that warrant close monitoring. The state's quality score of 62/100 ranks 30th nationally, falling in the "moderate" tier but trailing behind expansion peers and raising concerns about care outcomes relative to program size and spending efficiency. While Texas avoids the bottom-tier performance seen in neighboring southern states like Arkansas (54) and Oklahoma (51), its quality metrics suggest room for improvement in leveraging its extensive managed care network to drive better health outcomes. The combination of high managed care penetration with moderate quality scores presents both an opportunity and imperative for Texas to optimize its delivery system performance. Given ongoing federal regulatory activity around enrollment moratoria and quality measurement initiatives visible in recent Federal Register postings, Texas will need to balance continued cost efficiency with quality improvements while navigating the post-pandemic enrollment normalization period.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Michigan Medicaid Enrollment Drops 7.6% Year-Over-Year
alerts.description: Michigan experienced a significant enrollment decline of 7.6%, well above the 10% threshold for major changes.

alerts.title: High Spending States Show Quality Performance Disparities
alerts.description: Pennsylvania has the highest per-enrollee costs at $4,377 but quality scores are not proportionally high, suggesting inefficient spending.

alerts.title: Five States Score Below 55 on Quality Measures
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55/100 on quality measures, indicating serious care quality concerns.

alerts.title: Federal Enrollment Moratorium Announced for 2026
alerts.description: CMS has announced nationwide temporary enrollment moratoria that may impact future Medicaid program operations and enrollment patterns.

executiveInsights.title: Federal moratorium on Medicaid enrollment may impact Texas operations
executiveInsights.summary: Multiple Federal Register entries show CMS announcing nationwide temporary moratoriums on Medicaid enrollment in May 2026. This regulatory action could significantly disrupt current enrollment processes and system operations.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: An enrollment moratorium would immediately halt new member acquisitions and require rapid system reconfiguration, potentially triggering contract modifications and emergency operational changes for Accenture's deliverables.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment moratorium to HHSC leadership and assess contract impact

executiveInsights.title: Texas Medicaid enrollment declined 3.1% YoY to 4.03M members
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost approximately 129,000 Medicaid enrollees year-over-year, dropping to 4,032,864 total members. This decline aligns with the national trend but represents significant membership reduction.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines directly impact revenue projections and may signal the need for retention strategies or system capacity adjustments in our operational planning and client recommendations.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment trend implications with HHSC for capacity planning

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score at 62/100 ranks 30th nationally, below target
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62 out of 100 on quality measures, ranking 30th of 50 states and falling in the 'moderate' tier. This is significantly below high performers like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Poor quality scores expose Texas to federal penalties and create opportunities for Accenture to propose quality improvement initiatives that could drive additional contract value and demonstrate measurable impact.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Develop quality improvement proposal for HHSC leadership review

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee spending at $2,988 exceeds national average by $119
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, totaling $12.0B in expenditures. This represents a 4.2% premium over the national benchmark.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs indicate potential efficiency opportunities and cost management initiatives that Accenture could propose to demonstrate value and justify continued partnership with HHSC.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Analyze cost drivers and prepare efficiency recommendations for HHSC

executiveInsights.title: New CMS HCBS quality measures for 2028 may require system updates
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows CMS releasing new Home and Community-Based Services quality measure sets for 2028 implementation. These new requirements will likely necessitate system and reporting modifications.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures create implementation opportunities for Accenture while requiring proactive planning to ensure Texas systems can capture and report the required data by 2028 deadlines.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality measures and assess system readiness with HHSC

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropping 3.1% YoY could accelerate, reducing program scale and contract value.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop contingency plans for reduced scope and identify efficiency opportunities to maintain margins.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality improvement mandate
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating pressure for systematic improvements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement initiative targeting top-tier performance metrics.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration suggests upcoming major procurement cycles for plan contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position for managed care organization consulting opportunities and system modernization needs.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal enrollment moratorium
riskOpportunity.description: Nationwide temporary enrollment moratoriums could disrupt Texas operations and planning assumptions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor federal guidance closely and prepare operational adjustments for enrollment restrictions.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Home and Community-Based Services quality requirements create compliance and improvement needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop HCBS quality measurement and improvement capabilities ahead of 2028 requirements.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio and work plan items indicate heightened compliance scrutiny risk.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and proactively address potential audit findings.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost optimization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee cost below national average of $2,869 suggests efficiency pressure and optimization potential.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost optimization solutions while maintaining quality to help Texas sustain competitive positioning.

riskOpportunity.label: Texas procurement pipeline
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHSC procurement opportunities and ESBD portal indicate potential new contract vehicles emerging.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Actively monitor procurement portals and prepare competitive responses for emerging opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment decreased 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, reflecting post-pandemic eligibility normalization.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees, well above the national average of 83.2%.
